draft.introduction: 2 Kings 2:1–15 recounts Elijah’s ascension to heaven in a chariot of fire, and Elisha’s inheritance of a double portion of his spirit. Elijah’s departure without death made him a figure of eschatological expectation. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: When they had crossed, Elijah said to Elisha, “Ask what I shall do for you, before I am taken from you.” And Elisha said, “Please let there be a double portion of your spirit upon me.” And as they went on walking and talking, behold, a chariot of fire and horses of fire separated the two of them. And Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven. And Elisha saw it and cried, “My father, my father! The chariot of Israel and its horsemen!”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ διαβῆναι αὐτούς

translator_notes.note: Elijah’s departure without death (like Enoch in Genesis 5:24) established the expectation of his return before the Day of the Lord (Malachi 4:5). The New Testament identifies John the Baptist as Elijah’s return (Matthew 11:14), and Elijah appears alongside Moses at the Transfiguration.
